chapters.title: Seeing a rainbow after the storm
chapters.text:
Parenting is difficult; there is barely any time to catch your breath amidst worries and fears for your child. Taking full responsibility for another, more vulnerable person can take a toll on a caretaker. However, having a child on the queer spectrum poses a completely new challenge. Luckily, parenting an LGBTQ child is easier compared to past generations as the world is more welcoming of this community while more educational resources and support are available. Unfortunately, the latter is precisely what prevents parents from getting closer to their children.

Most caretakers have difficulty understanding the needs and challenges their LGBTQ child may experience. Searching for helpful and, most importantly, respectful information is a meaningful step toward a better relationship with their offspring.

Children shouldn’t reflect their parents; they should be their true selves.

Learning the cores of sexual minorities’ lives can help parents become better allies for their children. Some of these aspects include:

• Going public: what to do when your child finally comes out.
• Survival as a teenager: what you can do to help your LGBTQ kid through the challenges of school.
• Morale boosters: what you can do to support your LGBTQ child.
• Guidelines for parenting your transgender or genderqueer child.

A mother to a lesbian woman, Telaina Eriksen speaks from experience. She gently addresses all the questions a puzzled parent may have raising a gay child while staying practical.

Asking someone if they are gay should not be seen as a negative question. ~ Telaina Eriksen

So, if you’re ready to wave the rainbow flag with your kid next Pride Month, grab the handle now.

chapters.title: You reap what you sow
chapters.text:
Being gay is much easier and safer today than it used to be decades ago. To start with, sexuality or gender can no longer jeopardize a person's chances of work, at least in the US. There are also new role models for the LGBTQ community in media, sports, movies, and politics, creating more space for its members to express themselves confidently.

The age of coming out keeps getting younger as teenagers feel safer to confess their sexuality or gender. Nevertheless, many queer middle and high schoolers continue suffering from depression, self-harm, eating disorders, and STDs due to intolerance and lack of adult help. As of 2016, around 40 percent of queer adolescents faced bigotry in their communities; as for their parents, they found raising LGBTQ children challenging. Fortunately, the latter now have access to many useful materials to support their kids during their acceptance journey.

You can grieve the loss of the vision you had of your child, but don’t make them feel responsible.

Accepting a child’s sexuality comes with a set of rules. Surprise and shock are not forbidden when hearing the news, but this reaction can hurt your child unintentionally. The parental response is the litmus test for kids before opening up to the public, which can encourage or prevent them from coming out further.

When your child reveals their sexuality or gender, try to avoid questions that make them doubt their position or identity; “Are you sure?” is a good example of such. Other cautions include:

• Introducing conditions to your relationships
• Accidental outing
• A silent reaction to coming out

If you suspect your child is anywhere on the LGBTQ spectrum, you can prepare for the conversation in advance. Use questions like:

• “Do you want to tell me more about it?”
• “Do you want to discuss it together?”

While your child initiates the initial conversation, your mission is to continue the discussion to show support and genuine interest. Affirm your acceptance verbally to eliminate minimum misunderstanding, and suggest searching resources together. If you suspect your child may already have someone significant, address it gently to provide clarity and expertise only adults have. Finally, don’t blame yourself — homosexuality is not behavioral but biological, thus completely natural.

chapters.title: Golden stars for being “normal”
chapters.text:
Whoever said high school was the best time in a teenager's life was a shameless liar. Nothing is tougher than confining our kids to a universal educational system for years. Whether the school provides countless extracurriculars or doesn't even bother updating the syllabus, kids still experience age-related troubles like first love, puberty, or fear of not belonging. However, this snowball is twice as big for queer teenagers.

As their parent, you may feel a surge of new responsibilities that appear like a bonus level in the game of parenting. For that matter, proper self-management as an adult is an excellent initial support system for your child. Either way, you should stay open-minded to learn and adapt if necessary. This focus on personal development is essential as many parents tend to live through their children, which entails obsessive involvement in their activities. Such care puts them under pointless pressure. The “helicoptered” children fail to distinguish repairable mistakes, becoming overly stressed as a result.

Don’t let your child abuse their LGBTQ status as an excuse not to mature.

None of that means that school doesn't affect parents as well. A high-demanding curriculum can stretch your scholarly expectations, imposing a performance standard and amplifying the child's feeling of inadequacy. Unhealthy for every teenager, this scenario is particularly undesired for LGBTQ kids already living within the shame-pride framework.

Coming out to their families, queer teenagers expect a negative reaction, which is why verbal acceptance is crucial for their confidence. Subconsciously, they'll continue evaluating their actions on the “love-hate” scale, and your mission is to break it. Start with your own mind: your child's sexuality or gender is not a weakness and, thus, a prerequisite to demand more from them. Quite the opposite — it's a demand of you to be more vigilant:

• Accompany your trans child to the public toilet or ask a same-gender relative if possible.
• Research the laws that protect your child and refer to them when necessary.
• Have a sex talk regardless of their sexuality.
• Monitor prescribed drugs and alcohol at home — LGBTQ children are more susceptible to addictions because of constant societal pressure.

chapters.title: Looking for an ally in a flock of strangers
chapters.text:
People are social creatures, and community plays a fundamental role in building our self-image and sense of purpose. We feel validated if we have someone whose story is our current journey. For LGBTQ youth, community is vital; however, if a child is not out yet, they may need parents’ help searching for one.

The frustration that often comes with the inability to provide for all your child’s needs is normal; however, what you do with it determines your further relationship. If you’re a heterosexual parent, some aspects of homosexuality are naturally unfamiliar to you, so admitting your lack of knowledge is a first step toward improving your child’s social life.

Sometimes, parents are the first to hear the news about their child's sexuality or gender. If that’s your case, you agree to share the burden of the closet until your child feels safe to come out publicly. To alleviate this transition, help them find a reliable figure or community with a similar experience.

Sometimes, the best way to solve an issue is to share it with the right people.

Having LGBTQ family members or friends would be an excellent advantage for you and your child. This kind of connection will reduce the fear of coming out, but take precautions by asking for their permission.

Unfortunately, some parents can go overboard in their attempts to help by accidentally outing their child. Your seemingly innocent ask for a friend’s advice that wasn’t authorized can easily sow distrust between you and your child. Therefore, make asking for permission your default mode when helping them accommodate themselves as an LGBTQ person.

Getting assistance from people trained to work with teenagers — a school counselor or a psychologist — or those with an LGBTQ child is the next step; in a sense, you leave the family circle and enter the public one. The Internet is an obvious source of help; teenagers spend most of their time communicating online, so straddling this medium is smarter than opposing it due to “bad influence.”

The LGBTQ community has a massive online presence; immersing in it can help with three things:

• Having healthy control over your child’s Internet use
• Understanding your kid’s teenage mindset
• Finding a community for both you and them

Platforms like The Trevor Project, Tumblr, Wattpad, AO3, or TikTok may expand your vision of LGBTQ teenagers nowadays.

chapters.title: Parenting is being your child’s first advocate
chapters.text:
At all times, older people tend to devalue the efforts of today’s children. Generational conflicts may be as old as documented history, yet we can't deny the extreme circumstances our kids live in today. Climatic changes, a global pandemic, and the technological avalanche are surface-level examples of mental strain we all experience, yet this list is significantly longer for LGBTQ teenagers.

We live in a heteronormative society in which the prevailing group grants itself acts of microaggression like homophobic jokes, perpetuating stereotypes, and excluding LGBTQ people from communal activities. That being said, schools are perfect Petri dishes for these situations, filled with immature peers and old-guard teachers.

Unfortunately, your child is more likely than not to encounter a homophobic adult with decisive authority over them, such as teachers and school principals. The cumulative stress of all puns and pranks can overwhelm and discourage queer teenagers from standing their ground. This is where you enter the picture as a parent who must advocate for their child. For example, parents whose children have a disability constantly press for necessities for them. Despite different discrimination origins, LGBTQ and disabled kids are both affected as communities.

Advocating is correcting the imbalance of power that might occur in a situation where your child is at a disadvantage. ~ Telaina Eriksen

Make sure you check the following boxes when stepping into advocacy:

1. Gather information
2. Learn the rules
3. Listen and propose solutions
4. Engage in school life
5. Join or build a community of other parents with LGBTQ children

Collecting knowledge includes talking with your children, local LGBTQ figures, and family to test the waters, understand your community's attitude to queer people, and take advice for better parenting. The second step is learning the school's guidelines for filing complaints to address discrimination correctly. Listen and take notes during school meetings, and don't abuse the administration's patience with something small like getting angry with grades. Instead, make yourself recognizable and proactive.

Within your family, advocacy can take the form of:

• Ensuring your child's ready to come out to extended family
• Knowing which relatives to trust
• Reviewing homophobic incidents
• Putting your child first

Your battle isn't in vain if you make a path for others to walk.

Did you know? A 2018 survey revealed that nearly 17% of 180,000 undergraduate and graduate students identified as LGBTQ.

chapters.title: One color doesn’t make a rainbow
chapters.text:
Sexual orientation is a compelling part of a gay child’s life. Belonging to a minority can contribute to isolation and anxiety before the wide world, affecting a child’s career dreams and self-development. Yet, to the contrary, an LGBTQ label shouldn’t dominate their personality. Your child must have goals and interests independent from their sexuality, and your mission as a parent is to help them stay ambitious.

When your child has come out to you, they must avoid confining themselves to their sexuality. Kids can fall victim to discriminative stereotypes, refusing to engage in extracurriculars, volunteering, or part-time jobs in fear of confrontation. However, encouraging them to leave their comfort zone has three significant benefits:

• Presenting a society outside the school’s artificial environment
• Contributing to a sense of peer unity
• Widening opportunities to self-explore

On top of that, there are three key spheres you should help your kid navigate: friends and relationships, finances, and higher education.

• Friends

Hosting your kid’s friends at home gives a handy possibility to discover your child from a new perspective and note where they may need help. For example, some friendships are not as pure and long-lasting, and a parent can register this issue faster while letting their child handle it as a life experience. The comfort of your home will secure your child’s leisure and enable you to know them better. The same applies to romance.

A person is a sum of their parts; catering to one side gives a distorted picture.

• Financial independence

Make teaching your child the language of money a priority the moment they need to spend more than buying snacks. They should learn from their mistakes while still under your roof rather than making poor financial choices due to a lack of knowledge and responsibility. That said, the first year of college is an excellent time to set up a debit account for your child.

• What's next after high school?

When your child is ready to continue their education, research LGBTQ-friendly schools and scholarship funds. Encourage them to apply, but don’t force higher education if they’re not interested in a four-year course; there are multiple flexible options to study and work simultaneously.

Lastly, the critical condition you must sustain is responsibility. Delegate more of it to your child whenever they seem ready.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Nothing can motivate you to be a better parent than becoming one. Learning from practice may give you bruised knees, but also a more precious experience than any book. So, whenever you learn from your mistakes, be easier on your children. Not only do they have fewer years behind them, but also a vulnerable heart that loves who it loves. There is beauty in every challenge; learning to understand your gay child is an excellent exam in itself. While they have slightly different needs than straight kids, one thing they have in common is a need to be loved.

Confusion and frustration are natural reactions to your child’s coming out, so be honest in front of those anxious eyes. However, if you’re ready to join your kid on this strenuous path of acceptance and perseverance, you’re already a great parent. Don’t blame yourself for having expectations; you’d be guilty only when letting them overtake the bond between you and your out-of-the-closet child.

Despite a more positive attitude toward LGBTQ people, the latter still face difficulties at school and the workplace, undermining their social life and self-realization. Helping your child recognize a wider world outside the school setting is a crucial step toward a less fearful outlook; instead, it creates opportunities to realize their value beyond their sexuality. It may require some time and extra dedication on your part, but that is what unconditional love is. Love your child and navigate them toward a fulfilling future, and they’ll return the favor doubly.

Try this



• Learn the gay ABCs: ask your child to explain all the LGBTQ terms, or do your research together.
• Be active in the school microclimate: stay in contact with teachers, advocate for comprehensive sex education, and recognize the signs of bullying.

Read it till the end?
